_metadata.notes: The pons is divided into the basis pontis (ventral) containing pontine nuclei and corticospinal/corticopontine fibers, and the tegmentum (dorsal) containing cranial nerve nuclei, ascending/descending tracts, and reticular formation. The fourth ventricle forms the dorsal boundary. p-19 is a vascular territory overview figure (Figure 6-21) showing arterial patterns at three pontine levels with associated vascular syndromes.

anatomical_levels.description: Cross-section at the pontomedullary junction showing the transition from medulla to pons. The cerebellar peduncles (inferior and middle) are visible. Cerebellar cortex with deep nuclei is shown attached.

tracts.zone: basis pontis, scattered bundles among pontine nuclei
tracts.somatotopy: dispersed at this level; fibers intermixed with pontocerebellar fibers
tracts.vascular_territory: basilar artery paramedian and short circumferential branches
tracts.notes: At the pontomedullary junction the corticospinal fibers begin to scatter among pontine nuclei. They occupy the ventral portion of the basis pontis.

tracts.depth: mid (at junction of basis pontis and tegmentum)
tracts.somatotopy: at caudal pons: begins rotating from vertical (medial=leg) toward horizontal orientation
tracts.notes: The medial lemniscus is transitioning from its vertical orientation in the medulla to a more horizontal (mediolateral) orientation in the pons. Located in the ventral tegmentum just dorsal to the basis pontis.

tracts.depth: superficial to mid (lateral surface of tegmentum)
tracts.somatotopy: sacral fibers lateral, cervical fibers medial
tracts.notes: Located in the lateral tegmentum. Continues from medullary position. In lateral pontine lesions (AICA territory), this tract is affected causing contralateral pain/temperature loss.

tracts.somatotopy: onion-skin pattern: perioral represented rostrally, peripheral face caudally
tracts.notes: The spinal trigeminal tract and nucleus extend into the caudal pons. At this level, it represents the pars interpolaris portion.
The ICP carries spinocerebellar and olivocerebellar afferents into the cerebellum. Visible at caudal pontine level before it enters the cerebellum.

tracts.zone: lateral pons, forming the massive lateral expansion
tracts.vascular_territory: AICA / long circumferential branches of basilar
tracts.notes: The MCP is the largest cerebellar peduncle. It carries pontocerebellar fibers (from contralateral pontine nuclei). Begins to be visible at caudal pons.

tracts.zone: dorsal tegmentum, paramedian, just ventral to fourth ventricle floor
tracts.notes: The MLF runs as a compact bundle near the midline, just beneath the floor of the fourth ventricle. Carries internuclear fibers connecting CN3, CN4, CN6 nuclei.

tracts.vascular_territory: basilar artery paramedian and short circumferential branches
tracts.notes: The pontine reticular formation occupies much of the central tegmentum. Includes the caudal pontine reticular nucleus at this level.
Carries descending fibers from red nucleus to inferior olive, and ascending fibers. Located in the central tegmentum.

tracts.zone: lateral tegmentum, dorsolateral to spinothalamic tract
tracts.notes: The descending sympathetic fibers course in the lateral tegmentum, similar position to the medulla. Lesion causes ipsilateral Horner syndrome.

anatomical_levels.level_name: Caudal Pons (CN6 and CN7 level - facial colliculus)
anatomical_levels.description: Cross-section showing the facial colliculus region. CN7 fibers loop around CN6 nucleus (internal genu of facial nerve). This is the key level for Millard-Gubler and Foville syndromes.

tracts.vascular_territory: basilar artery paramedian and short circumferential branches
tracts.notes: Corticospinal fibers scattered in basis pontis. Basilar paramedian branch occlusion causes contralateral hemiparesis.

tracts.zone: dorsal tegmentum, paramedian, beneath facial colliculus in fourth ventricle floor
tracts.notes: CN6 nucleus is located just deep to the floor of the fourth ventricle, beneath the facial colliculus. Contains both abducens motor neurons AND internuclear neurons that project via MLF to contralateral CN3. Key structure in Foville syndrome.

tracts.somatotopy: dorsal subnucleus = forehead (bilateral cortical input); lateral subnucleus = lower face (contralateral cortical input only)
tracts.notes: CN7 motor nucleus is in the ventrolateral tegmentum. Its axons first course dorsomedially to loop around CN6 nucleus (internal genu), then exit ventrolaterally. This long intrapontine course makes it vulnerable. Lesion here = LMN facial palsy (entire ipsilateral face).

tracts.zone: courses from ventrolateral tegmentum -> dorsomedially around CN6 nucleus -> ventrolaterally to exit pons
tracts.vascular_territory: paramedian branches (genu around CN6) + AICA (exit zone)
tracts.notes: The internal genu of CN7 wraps around the CN6 nucleus creating the facial colliculus. This is the anatomical basis for combined CN6+CN7 palsy in pontine lesions. Millard-Gubler syndrome: CN6 + CN7 palsy + contralateral hemiparesis.

tracts.somatotopy: rotating: leg fibers shifting lateral, arm fibers medial
tracts.vascular_territory: basilar artery paramedian branches (medial part) + short circumferential (lateral part)
tracts.notes: At caudal pons level, the medial lemniscus has rotated from its vertical medullary orientation and is becoming more horizontal. Located at the junction of tegmentum and basis pontis.

tracts.somatotopy: sacral fibers lateral, cervical fibers medial
tracts.notes: Continues in lateral tegmentum. Affected in lateral pontine syndrome.

tracts.zone: dorsal tegmentum, paramedian, just ventral to CN6 nucleus
tracts.notes: Runs immediately adjacent to and ventral to CN6 nucleus. Carries internuclear fibers. Lesion causes INO (internuclear ophthalmoplegia). In Foville syndrome, both CN6 and MLF are affected.

tracts.zone: paramedian tegmentum, ventral and slightly caudal to CN6 nucleus
tracts.notes: The PPRF is the horizontal gaze center. Located near CN6 nucleus. Lesion causes ipsilateral horizontal gaze palsy. Combined CN6 nucleus + PPRF lesion = Foville syndrome (gaze palsy + ipsilateral facial palsy + contralateral hemiparesis).
Part of the auditory pathway. Located in ventrolateral tegmentum near CN7 nucleus. Receives bilateral input from cochlear nuclei.
Crossing auditory fibers from cochlear nuclei to contralateral superior olive and lateral lemniscus. Located in the ventral tegmentum at this level.

tracts.zone: lateral tegmentum, dorsal to spinothalamic tract
tracts.notes: Ascending auditory pathway from superior olivary complex and cochlear nuclei to inferior colliculus. Begins to form at this caudal pontine level.

tracts.vascular_territory: AICA / long circumferential branches of basilar
tracts.notes: Very prominent at this level. Carries pontocerebellar fibers.

tracts.zone: lateral tegmentum, within lateral reticular area
tracts.notes: Continues in lateral tegmentum. AICA territory lesion can cause Horner syndrome.
At this level includes the caudal and oral pontine reticular nuclei.
Carries descending rubro-olivary fibers. Lesion can cause palatal myoclonus (via disruption of Guillain-Mollaret triangle).

anatomical_levels.level_name: Mid Pons (CN5 entry zone, CN7 exiting)
anatomical_levels.description: Cross-section at mid-pontine level showing the trigeminal nerve entry/exit zone. The principal (main) sensory nucleus of CN5 and motor nucleus of CN5 are visible. The facial nerve (CN7) exits ventrolaterally at this level.

tracts.somatotopy: dispersed among pontine nuclei and transverse pontocerebellar fibers
tracts.vascular_territory: basilar artery paramedian and short circumferential branches
tracts.notes: The basis pontis is at its widest at mid-pontine levels. CST fibers are broken into many small bundles.

tracts.zone: lateral tegmentum, medial to principal sensory nucleus
tracts.notes: Motor nucleus of CN5 innervates muscles of mastication (masseter, temporalis, pterygoids). Located medial to the principal sensory nucleus.

tracts.zone: lateral tegmentum, at the entry zone of trigeminal nerve
tracts.somatotopy: somatotopic representation of face: mandibular division ventral, ophthalmic division dorsal
tracts.vascular_territory: AICA / SCA territory boundary (long circumferential branches)
tracts.notes: The principal sensory nucleus mediates discriminative touch of the face. Located at the pontine entry of CN5 root. Large nucleus that serves as a landmark for mid-pontine level.

tracts.zone: entering/exiting from lateral surface of mid pons
tracts.notes: The large trigeminal root is the major surface landmark of the mid pons. It enters/exits the lateral pons at the junction of the basis pontis and middle cerebellar peduncle.

tracts.somatotopy: now horizontal: leg lateral, arm medial
tracts.vascular_territory: basilar paramedian (medial portion) + short circumferential (lateral portion)
tracts.notes: At mid-pons, the medial lemniscus has fully rotated to horizontal orientation. Leg representation is lateral, arm medial. This orientation persists to the midbrain.

tracts.somatotopy: sacral fibers lateral, cervical fibers medial
tracts.notes: At mid-pons, the STT is in the lateral tegmentum near the trigeminal structures. At this level, the vascular supply transitions between AICA and SCA territories.
Continues in same position just beneath fourth ventricle floor.

tracts.zone: lateral tegmentum, dorsal to spinothalamic tract
tracts.notes: The lateral lemniscus is well formed at mid-pons. Ascending to inferior colliculus.

tracts.zone: dorsolateral tegmentum, forming the lateral wall of the fourth ventricle
tracts.notes: The SCP begins to be prominent at mid-pontine levels. It carries the major cerebellar efferent output (from dentate nucleus) destined for the red nucleus and thalamus.
Still very prominent at this level.

tracts.zone: basis pontis, scattered throughout between CST bundles
tracts.vascular_territory: basilar artery paramedian and short circumferential
tracts.notes: Pontine nuclei receive corticopontine input and send fibers via MCP to contralateral cerebellum. Prominent at this level.
Continues in lateral tegmentum.
Oral pontine reticular nucleus. Part of ascending reticular activating system.
Continues in central tegmentum.

anatomical_levels.level_name: Mid Pons (upper, CN5 motor/sensory nuclei prominent)
anatomical_levels.description: Cross-section at upper mid-pontine level. CN5 motor and principal sensory nuclei clearly visible. The mesencephalic nucleus of CN5 begins to appear. Basis pontis remains wide with scattered CST bundles.

tracts.vascular_territory: basilar artery paramedian and short circumferential branches
tracts.notes: Basis pontis still prominent at this level.

tracts.zone: basis pontis; frontopontine medial, parietotemporooccipital lateral
tracts.vascular_territory: basilar artery paramedian (medial fibers) + short circumferential (lateral fibers)
tracts.notes: Corticopontine fibers accompany the CST in the basis pontis. They synapse on pontine nuclei which project to contralateral cerebellum via MCP.
Motor nucleus of CN5 at its most prominent level. Supplies muscles of mastication.

tracts.zone: lateral tegmentum, lateral to motor nucleus
tracts.notes: Principal sensory nucleus. Discriminative touch of face.

tracts.zone: lateral periaqueductal / periventricular, lateral to fourth ventricle
tracts.notes: Unique: contains primary sensory neuron cell bodies WITHIN the CNS (not in ganglion). Mediates proprioception from muscles of mastication and periodontal ligaments. Extends from mid-pons to midbrain.
Horizontal orientation maintained. At basis-tegmentum junction.
At upper mid-pons, the vascular territory shifts more toward SCA.
Well-formed at this level, ascending toward inferior colliculus.

tracts.zone: dorsolateral tegmentum, forming lateral wall of fourth ventricle
tracts.notes: SCP is prominent at this level, beginning to converge toward the midline as it ascends toward its decussation in the caudal midbrain.
Same position beneath fourth ventricle floor.

tracts.zone: dorsolateral tegmentum, near SCP and fourth ventricle floor
tracts.notes: Major noradrenergic nucleus. Located in the dorsolateral tegmentum near the SCP. Visible as pigmented neurons in fresh specimens.
At upper mid-pons, now within SCA territory.
Oral pontine reticular nucleus and other reticular nuclei.
Continues in central tegmentum.

anatomical_levels.level_name: Rostral Pons (SCP prominent, trigeminal sensory nucleus upper)
anatomical_levels.description: Cross-section at rostral pontine level. The superior cerebellar peduncles are converging toward their decussation. The basis pontis begins to narrow. The fourth ventricle is narrowing.

tracts.zone: basis pontis, becoming more compact as basis narrows
tracts.vascular_territory: basilar artery paramedian and short circumferential branches (SCA territory)
tracts.notes: At rostral pons, the basis pontis narrows and CST fibers begin to reconverge before entering the cerebral peduncle.

The SCP is the dominant structure at rostral pons. It forms the lateral walls of the upper fourth ventricle and is converging before its decussation in the caudal midbrain. SCA occlusion affects this structure.
Horizontal orientation. At rostral pons, the lateral portion enters SCA territory.
At rostral pons, clearly in SCA territory.

tracts.zone: lateral tegmentum, superficial, ascending toward inferior colliculus
tracts.notes: Ascending to inferior colliculus. At this level it includes the nucleus of the lateral lemniscus.
At rostral pons, the vascular supply transitions to SCA territory.
The rostral extent of PPRF. Horizontal gaze center fibers project to ipsilateral CN6 nucleus (caudally).
Continues in central tegmentum.
Oral pontine reticular nucleus. Important for arousal - bilateral rostral pontine tegmental lesions can cause coma.
In SCA territory at this level.

tracts.tract_name: Mesencephalic nucleus and tract of CN5
tracts.zone: lateral periventricular, along lateral edge of fourth ventricle
tracts.notes: Extends rostrally along the lateral edge of the fourth ventricle / periaqueductal gray.
Most prominent at rostral pons. Major noradrenergic center.

anatomical_levels.description: Cross-section at the pontomesencephalic junction. The basis pontis is narrowing into the cerebral peduncle. The SCP fibers are converging and beginning to decussate. The fourth ventricle is transitioning to the cerebral aqueduct. The substantia nigra begins to appear.

tracts.zone: ventral, compacting from scattered basis pontis into crus cerebri
tracts.somatotopy: reconverging into somatotopic order: face lateral, arm middle, leg medial (in peduncle)
tracts.notes: The CST fibers are compacting as the basis pontis gives way to the crus cerebri. The transition is gradual.
SCP fibers converge and begin their decussation (decussation of SCP = Wernekinck's commissure). After crossing, they project to contralateral red nucleus and thalamus (VL).

tracts.zone: lateral tegmentum (shifting laterally from ventral tegmentum position)
tracts.notes: At the pontomesencephalic junction, the medial lemniscus shifts laterally and will be located in the lateral tegmentum in the midbrain.
At this level, STT and medial lemniscus are converging in the lateral tegmentum.

tracts.zone: lateral tegmentum, superficial, approaching inferior colliculus
tracts.notes: Approaching the inferior colliculus where most fibers will synapse.
Transitioning from fourth ventricle floor to periaqueductal position.

tracts.zone: central tegmentum, between SCP and medial lemniscus
tracts.notes: Sandwiched between converging SCP fibers.
Pontine reticular formation. Crucial for consciousness at this level.
SCA territory. Lesion causes ipsilateral Horner.

tracts.zone: dorsal tegmentum, paramedian, ventral to aqueduct at pontomesencephalic junction
tracts.notes: CN4 nucleus appears at the pontomesencephalic junction. Its fibers uniquely decussate dorsally and exit from the dorsal surface of the brainstem.

anatomical_levels.level_name: Arterial Territories within the Pons with Vascular Syndromes
anatomical_levels.description: Overview figure showing three pontine levels (caudal, mid, rostral) with color-coded arterial territories and associated clinical vascular syndromes. Pink/red shading indicates typical infarct territories.

clinical_features: Ipsilateral CN7 LMN palsy (at caudal pons, if genu/fascicle involved)

vascular_territories.coverage: Lateral tegmentum + middle cerebellar peduncle at CAUDAL pons
clinical_features: Ipsilateral facial sensory loss (spinal CN5 tract/nucleus)
Ipsilateral lateral gaze palsy (if extends medially)

vascular_territories.coverage: Lateral tegmentum + SCP at ROSTRAL pons
structures_supplied: Medial lemniscus (lateral portion at rostral pons)
clinical_features: Contralateral hearing loss (lateral lemniscus, bilateral pathway so rarely isolated)
Ipsilateral loss of facial proprioception (mesencephalic CN5)

clinical_triad: Ipsilateral CN6 palsy (lateral rectus weakness → medial gaze deviation)
syndrome_details.notes: Classic medial caudal pontine syndrome. The lesion typically affects the ventral pons where CN6 and CN7 fascicles exit alongside the CST.

clinical_features: Ipsilateral horizontal gaze palsy (CN6 nucleus + PPRF — eyes deviate AWAY from lesion, toward hemiparesis)
syndrome_details.notes: Distinguished from Millard-Gubler by the gaze palsy (nucleus involvement, not just fascicle). In Foville, the eyes deviate away from the lesion side because CN6 nucleus = final common pathway for ipsilateral horizontal gaze.

clinical_features: Vertical eye movements preserved (CN3 nucleus in midbrain, above lesion)
syndrome_details.notes: Caused by bilateral ventral pontine lesion (basis pontis). The reticular formation in the tegmentum is spared, so consciousness is preserved. Communication possible via vertical eye movements and blinking.

anatomical_levels.description: Cross-section at the isthmus (junction of pons and midbrain). The cerebral aqueduct replaces the fourth ventricle. The SCP is decussating. The substantia nigra is becoming visible. The basis pontis transitions into the crus cerebri.

tracts.zone: ventral, forming the crus cerebri (cerebral peduncle base)
tracts.vascular_territory: SCA / PCA (posterior cerebral artery) paramedian branches
tracts.notes: The transition from basis pontis to crus cerebri is nearly complete. CST fibers are becoming compacted.

The decussation of the SCP (Wernekinck's commissure). After crossing, fibers ascend to the contralateral red nucleus and VL thalamus. This is why cerebellar lesions cause IPSILATERAL ataxia (double-cross: SCP crosses here, then cortex to cord crosses at pyramids).
Now positioned laterally in the tegmentum, as it will be in the midbrain.
In the lateral tegmentum. Converging with medial lemniscus at this level.
Now adjacent to the cerebral aqueduct. CN4 nucleus is nearby.
CN4 nucleus at the isthmus. Its fibers decussate dorsally.
The rostral edge of substantia nigra appears at the pontomesencephalic junction.
Approaching the inferior colliculus at the pontomesencephalic junction.
Continues in central tegmentum.
Pedunculopontine nucleus region. Critical for arousal.
SCA territory.
